Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd (PCL) was incorporated in Singapore on 2015-03-27 as a company in the business of leasing cars.
PCL conducted its business at the office of Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd (PCC) and kept its files and records at PCC's office and in PCC's office computers.
PCL had no staff of its own and used PCC's staff for its business operations.
PCL obtained loan facilities from Maybank Banking Berhad and DBS Bank Ltd to fund the purchase of cars that were leased to Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd and other customers.
PCL entered into leasing arrangements under which Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd took possession of cars owned by PCL while ownership of the cars remained with PCL.

Under a back-to-back arrangement, payments that Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd received from its hirers were paid to PCL, and PCL in turn paid Maybank Banking Berhad and DBS Bank Ltd for the mortgage on the cars and other operating expenses such as road tax and insurance premiums.
PCL leased 23 cars to Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd at rates that were cheaper than market rates.
By May 2018, PCL had a fleet of 64 cars, of which 23 cars were leased to Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd.
From May 2018, Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd stopped paying PCL the monthly rentals for the 23 cars it had leased from PCL, with total monthly rentals of about $60,000.

Following the cessation of monthly rental payments by Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd, PCL was unable to pay instalments due under its loan facilities with Maybank Banking Berhad and DBS Bank Ltd and other expenses such as insurance premiums for the 23 cars.
Between July 2018 and October 2018, PCL sold 18 cars, including 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd and four cars to SKL Automobile Pte Ltd and Fu Ee Cars Pte Ltd.
Between 2018-07-26 and 2018-08-23, PCL sold 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd for a total price of $1,085,000.

From the sale of the 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd, a portion of the sale proceeds was used to pay off outstanding loans to Maybank Banking Berhad and DBS Bank Ltd, and a balance amount of $291,373.22 was due to PCL.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the sale of 14 cars owned by PCL to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd and the transfer of $289,700.47 of balance sale proceeds to the personal bank account of Lim Siew Ling, in the context of PCL's financing arrangements and leasing structure, resulted in a shortfall to PCL compared to the amounts that would have been available to PCL if the cars had been sold and proceeds retained in PCL's accounts.
Party_Details.Rule: A company director must manage company assets and transactions so that they are commercially justifiable from the company's perspective, taking into account the company's financial position, existing financing arrangements, and the nature of counterparties, and to ensure that company funds and sale proceeds of company assets are applied for company purposes rather than diverted to personal accounts, unless there is a documented basis for reimbursement or remuneration.
Party_Details.Application: PCL operated a leasing business funded by loan facilities from Maybank Banking Berhad and DBS Bank Ltd, with a back-to-back flow of rental payments from Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd to PCL and then to the banks. In mid-2018, Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd stopped paying approximately $60,000 per month in rentals for 23 cars, while PCL remained responsible for loan instalments and operating expenses. Between July and August 2018, PCL sold 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd for $1,085,000, used part of the proceeds to discharge bank loans, and arranged for $1,672.75 of the balance to be paid to PCL and $289,700.47 to be paid to the personal bank account of Lim Siew Ling. These transfers occurred while PCL's bank accounts showed low balances or overdrafts and while PCL still had obligations on remaining cars. The structure of these transactions created a situation where a substantial portion of net sale proceeds from PCL-owned assets was held outside PCL's bank accounts.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed whether Lim Siew Ling, as sole director of PCL at the material time, acted honestly and with reasonable diligence in causing PCL to sell 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd and in arranging for $289,700.47 of balance sale proceeds to be transferred to her personal bank account. It held that under s 157A of the Companies Act and PCL's governance structure, management powers rested with the board, and Lim, as sole director, had authority to sell the cars without needing consent from the Neo sisters as shareholders. The court rejected the plaintiff's contention that Lim lacked authority because she was only a de facto 40% shareholder, noting that share regularisation occurred after the sales and that shareholders cannot ratify or negate statutory director duties where criminal sanctions may apply.

On pricing, the court compared Zenith's total purchase price of $1,085,000 with figures advanced by Heng Hong Hing for hypothetical direct sales and accepted evidence from both Heng and Zenith's director, Tan Teck Ann, that dealer prices are typically 5–10% below open market prices. It found that the prices paid by Zenith were consistent with dealer market prices, that Lim had obtained comparative quotes from other dealers, and that some cars were in fact sold to SKL Automobile Pte Ltd and Fu Ee Cars Pte Ltd, which undermined any suggestion of a special arrangement with Zenith. The court concluded that the 14 cars were not sold at undervalue and that the transactions were conducted at arm's length.

Regarding the transfer of the 13 cars' balance sale proceeds to Lim's personal account, the court examined PCL's financial position in mid-2018. Bank statements showed minimal balances or overdrafts in PCL's DBS and Maybank accounts, while loan instalments under the Maybank and DBS facilities remained due and Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd had stopped paying rentals on 23 cars. The court accepted that if the net sale proceeds had been paid into PCL's accounts, the banks could have set off the funds against overdue loan instalments, leaving PCL without liquidity to pay operating expenses such as insurance, road tax, repairs and other obligations. Lim was also a guarantor under the loan facilities. The court found that Lim reasonably anticipated ongoing obligations for 10 unrepossessed cars and other expenses and that transferring a substantial buffer to her personal account was a commercially justifiable way to preserve liquidity for PCL's operations.

The court then scrutinised Lim's use of the funds. It found that she paid $47,213.64 towards PCL's loan facilities, road tax and miscellaneous expenses, $32,130.13 for insurance premiums, $6,660 for towing charges, and refunded $51,060 in security deposits to lessees, less $6,042 already reimbursed, totalling $131,021.77. The court accepted documentary support for these payments. It considered whether Lim should have forfeited three security deposits totalling $45,500 and claimed additional lease payments upon early termination. Lim's explanation that the lessees were long-term repeat customers who generated ongoing business and referrals for the Prime Cars Group was accepted as commercially sensible. The court held that a director may take a broader view of the company's interests and that returning deposits and not pursuing further sums in these circumstances was commercially justifiable and consistent with PCL's long-term interests.

The court noted that Lim also claimed that the remaining balance of the $289,700.47 represented unpaid salary and transport allowance from PCL, forming part of her counterclaim. It treated this as a separate remuneration issue and not as evidence of misappropriation. The court rejected the plaintiff's reliance on two transfers from PCL's DBS account to Lim's personal account ($5,516 and $4,000) as indicative of dishonesty, given the broader pattern of Lim funding PCL's expenses from her own resources during the liquidity crisis.

The court was critical of the conduct of the Neo sisters and Heng Hong Hing, finding that they instructed Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd to stop paying rentals to PCL and concealed 10 cars, fully aware that PCL depended on those rentals to service its bank loans. It characterised their actions as the cause of PCL's liquidity crisis and noted that they later used the resulting financial distress as the basis for claims against Lim. Nonetheless, the court confined its legal analysis to whether Lim's conduct met the statutory and fiduciary standards and concluded that she acted honestly and with reasonable diligence in managing PCL's affairs during the crisis.

On the dishonest assistance claim against Zenith, the court applied the elements from George Raymond Zage III v Ho Chi Kwong, requiring a breach of fiduciary duty, assistance, and dishonesty. Having found no breach by Lim, the claim failed at the threshold. In any event, the court held that Zenith's director, Tan, did not know Lim before the transactions, negotiated at market dealer prices, and only agreed to pay the balance proceeds to Lim personally after conducting an ACRA search confirming that she was PCL's sole director and shareholder and that there were no charges on PCL. The court found no evidence of assistance in any breach or of dishonesty.

On knowing receipt, the court applied the elements from Ho Chi Kwong, including disposal of assets in breach of fiduciary duty and knowledge that the assets derived from such a breach. It held that because the cars were sold at market value and there was no breach by Lim, there was no improper disposal of assets and no basis to conclude that Zenith received any benefit traceable to a breach. The claim for knowing receipt was therefore dismissed.

On conspiracy by unlawful means, the court referred to EFT Holdings and subsequent authorities, requiring a combination, intention to injure, unlawful acts, acts in furtherance, and resulting loss. It found no evidence of any agreement, express or implied, between Lim and Tan to injure PCL, and reiterated that there was no underlying unlawful act because Lim had not breached her duties. The conspiracy claim was dismissed as baseless.

The court therefore dismissed PCL's claims in their entirety and, as a consequence, did not need to determine the merits of Lim's third party claims against Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd, Heng Hong Hing and the Neo sisters. It proceeded to consider Lim's counterclaim for reimbursement of expenses and remuneration, finding that she was entitled to reimbursement of undisputed expenses and addressing separately the disputed components relating to security deposits and director's salary and transport allowance.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd is a Singapore-incorporated company in the business of buying and selling used cars.
Between 26 July 2018 and 23 August 2018, Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd purchased 14 cars from Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd for a total consideration of $1,085,000.
From the sale of the 14 Cars, a sum of $291,373.22 was supposed to be paid by Zenith to PCL after deduction of the outstanding loans to the Banks for these cars.
Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd requested that $1,672.75 be transferred to PCL for the sale of vehicle SLK7529X and that the remaining $289,700.47 be transferred to Lim Siew Ling's personal bank account.

For at least one of the early transactions, the purchase agreement for vehicle SJQ8877B recorded a cash amount of $8,500 as a deposit with a handwritten note indicating it was to be refunded to the owner.

Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd, through its director Tan Teck Ann, conducted an ACRA search which showed that Lim Siew Ling was the sole director and shareholder of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and that there were no charges registered against Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd.
For the first three cars sold to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd, its director inspected and test drove the cars and negotiated prices with Lim Siew Ling.
For 11 repossessed cars purchased from Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd, Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd did not receive car keys and was unable to test drive or inspect the interior.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd's purchase of 14 cars from Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and its payment of $289,700.47 of balance sale proceeds to the personal bank account of Lim Siew Ling exposed Zenith to the risk that it had paid less than open market value for the cars or directed funds away from Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd in a manner inconsistent with the company's internal allocation of sale proceeds.
Party_Details.Rule: A commercial purchaser of company-owned assets is generally to pay prices consistent with prevailing dealer market values and to direct payment of sale proceeds in accordance with the seller company's payment instructions, having taken reasonable steps such as checking public corporate records to confirm the authority of the person giving instructions.
Party_Details.Application: Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd purchased 14 cars from Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd in July and August 2018 for $1,085,000. For the first three cars, Zenith's director inspected and test drove the vehicles and negotiated prices; for the remaining repossessed cars, Zenith priced them without test drives, reflecting the additional risk. Zenith then applied part of the purchase price directly to discharge bank loans on the cars and held a balance of $291,373.22, of which $1,672.75 was paid to Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and $289,700.47 was paid to the personal account of Lim Siew Ling. Before making payments to Lim's personal account, Zenith conducted an ACRA search confirming that she was the sole director and shareholder of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and that there were no registered charges. Zenith followed the payment instructions of the person recorded as having full corporate authority.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered whether Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd had dishonestly assisted any breach of duty by Lim Siew Ling or had received assets with knowledge of a breach. It first determined that Lim had not breached her fiduciary duties in relation to the sale of the 14 cars or the handling of the sale proceeds. On that basis, the dishonest assistance and knowing receipt claims against Zenith failed at the outset because both causes of action require an underlying breach of fiduciary duty.

The court nonetheless examined Zenith's conduct. It accepted evidence that Zenith's director, Tan Teck Ann, did not know Lim before the transactions and that the prices paid for the 14 cars were consistent with dealer market prices, taking into account that dealer prices are typically 5–10% below open market retail prices and that some of the cars were repossessed and could not be test driven. The court found no evidence of any special discount or preferential pricing that would suggest Zenith obtained the cars at a substantial undervalue.

Regarding the payment of $289,700.47 to Lim's personal bank account, the court found that Tan initially hesitated when Lim requested that balance proceeds be paid to her personally. Tan then conducted an ACRA search, which showed that Lim was the sole director and shareholder of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and that there were no registered charges over the company. Satisfied by this public information, Tan followed Lim's instructions and issued cheques to her personal account. The court held that this sequence of actions demonstrated that Zenith took reasonable steps to verify Lim's authority and that there were no circumstances so unusual or contrary to accepted commercial practice as to put Zenith on notice of any irregularity.

Applying the test for dishonest assistance from George Raymond Zage III v Ho Chi Kwong, the court found no evidence that Zenith rendered assistance to any breach of duty by Lim, nor that it acted dishonestly. Zenith's role was limited to purchasing cars at market dealer prices and paying the agreed consideration in the manner instructed by the person publicly recorded as having authority to act for the seller. Under the knowing receipt framework, the court found that Zenith did not receive any benefit traceable to a breach of fiduciary duty because the cars were acquired at market value and there was no underlying breach. The court also found no basis to conclude that Zenith had knowledge, actual or constructive, of any breach or that it would be unconscionable for Zenith to retain the benefit of the transactions.

On the conspiracy claim, the court held that there was no evidence of any agreement, express or implied, between Lim and Tan to injure Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd, and no unlawful act on which a conspiracy could be founded. The court emphasised that Zenith's conduct was consistent with ordinary commercial practice and that Zenith had no involvement in the internal disputes between Lim and the Neo sisters. Accordingly, all claims against Zenith were dismissed and Zenith was found not liable.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Siew Ling

Facts.Text: Lim Siew Ling is the wife of the late Neo Nam Kah, who died in October 2013.
After the death of Neo Nam Kah in October 2013, Lim Siew Ling inherited his shares in Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd.
Following the death of Neo Nam Kah, Lim Siew Ling was appointed as a director of Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd alongside Neo Choon Sian and Neo Yan.
On the incorporation of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd on 2015-03-27, Lim Siew Ling became its sole director and sole registered shareholder of 1,000 shares.

In 2015, Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd issued an additional 100,000 shares in the name of Lim Siew Ling, while there was an internal understanding that the economic shareholding would be 40% for Lim Siew Ling, 35% for Neo Choon Sian, and 25% for Neo Yan.
By 2017, Lim Siew Ling was the sole director of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and a director of Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd.
From early 2017 to mid-2017, Lim Siew Ling drew a monthly salary of $8,000 from Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd, which was later increased to $10,000 per month.
In May 2018, the monthly salary of $10,000 that Lim Siew Ling had been receiving from Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd was stopped.

In May 2018, Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd, whose director was Heng Hong Hing, stopped paying monthly rentals to Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd for 23 cars, following instructions from Neo Choon Sian and Neo Yan, without informing Lim Siew Ling.

By July 2018, Lim Siew Ling, as director of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd, was dealing with a situation where Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd had low or overdrawn balances in its bank accounts with DBS Bank Ltd and Maybank Banking Berhad while loan instalments and operating expenses remained payable.

Between 2018-07-26 and 2018-08-23, acting as director of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd, Lim Siew Ling arranged the sale of 14 cars owned by Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd for $1,085,000.
In August 2018, Lim Siew Ling directed the repossession of 13 of the 23 cars that Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd had leased to Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd.

After payment of outstanding loans to Maybank Banking Berhad and DBS Bank Ltd from the sale proceeds of the 14 cars, Lim Siew Ling instructed Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd to transfer $1,672.75 of the remaining balance to Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and $289,700.47, being the balance sale proceeds for 13 cars, to her personal bank account.

By August 2018, the use of a company car, a Toyota Alphard, previously available to Lim Siew Ling through Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd, had been withdrawn and the vehicle was removed from her possession.

On 2018-10-12, Lim Siew Ling transferred her registered shares in Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and Prime Cars Credit Pte Ltd to the Neo sisters so that the shareholding in Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd reflected a 40:35:25 split among Lim Siew Ling, Neo Choon Sian, and Neo Yan.
On 2019-01-16, Lim Siew Ling ceased to be a director of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd when she was removed from that position.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Lim Siew Ling's actions as sole director of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd in repossessing 13 leased cars, selling 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd, directing $289,700.47 of balance sale proceeds to her personal bank account, and using those funds alongside her own resources to pay Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd's loan instalments, operating expenses, and customer refunds, resulted in a net personal benefit to her beyond documented reimbursements and claimed remuneration.
Party_Details.Rule: A person who controls a company's bank mandates and asset disposals is to ensure that proceeds from the sale of company assets are applied to meet the company's obligations and documented entitlements, and that any amounts retained personally are supported by clear records of reimbursement or remuneration, so that there is no unexplained surplus retained outside the company.
Party_Details.Application: As sole director and registered sole shareholder of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd during the material period, Lim Siew Ling arranged the sale of 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd for $1,085,000 and instructed that, after discharging bank loans, $1,672.75 be paid to Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and $289,700.47 be paid to her personal bank account. At the same time, Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd's bank accounts showed low balances or overdrafts, and the company faced ongoing loan instalments and operating expenses, while rental inflows from 23 cars leased to Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd had ceased. Lim used her personal account to pay $131,021.77 on behalf of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd for loan instalments, insurance premiums, road tax, towing charges, and refunds of security deposits. The pattern of transactions shows that a substantial part of the net sale proceeds was applied to company-related expenses, while the balance was treated by Lim as remuneration.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court examined whether Lim Siew Ling, as sole director of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd, breached her fiduciary duties by repossessing 13 cars from Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd, selling 14 cars to Zenith Automobile Pte Ltd, and directing $289,700.47 of balance sale proceeds to her personal bank account. Applying s 157(1) and s 157A of the Companies Act and the common law standards in cases such as Ho Kang Peng v Scintronix and Goh Chan Peng v Beyonics, the court considered both the subjective and objective elements of the duty to act honestly and with reasonable diligence.

On authority, the court held that management powers under s 157A rest with the board, and that Lim, as sole director of Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd at the material time, had authority to sell the company's cars. The fact that there was an internal understanding that she held only 40% of the economic interest did not affect her formal authority as director. The court rejected the argument that she required consent from the Neo sisters as de facto majority shareholders, noting that shareholders cannot authorise or ratify conduct that would contravene statutory director duties carrying criminal sanctions.

On pricing, the court assessed whether the 14 cars were sold at undervalue. It compared Zenith's total purchase price of $1,085,000 with figures advanced by Heng Hong Hing for hypothetical direct sales and accepted evidence from both Heng and Zenith's director that dealer prices are typically 5–10% below open market retail prices. The court found that the prices paid by Zenith were consistent with dealer market values, that Lim had obtained comparative quotes from other dealers, and that some cars were sold to SKL Automobile Pte Ltd and Fu Ee Cars Pte Ltd, indicating that there was no exclusive or preferential arrangement with Zenith. The court concluded that the 14 cars were not sold at undervalue and that Lim's decision to sell to a dealer rather than to individual buyers was commercially justified given the need for quick realisation of funds and the additional costs and delays associated with retail sales.

On the transfer of $289,700.47 to Lim's personal account, the court examined Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd's financial position in mid-2018. Bank statements showed minimal balances or overdrafts in the company's DBS and Maybank accounts, while loan instalments under the Maybank and DBS facilities remained due. Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd had stopped paying rentals on 23 cars, and 10 of those cars could not be repossessed because their locations were concealed. The court accepted that if the net sale proceeds had been paid into Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd's bank accounts, the banks could have set off the funds against overdue loan instalments, leaving the company without liquidity to pay operating expenses such as insurance, road tax, and repairs. Lim was also a guarantor under the loan facilities, exposing her to personal liability if the company defaulted.

The court found that Lim used $131,021.77 of the $289,700.47 to pay Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd's obligations, including $47,213.64 for loan instalments, road tax, and miscellaneous expenses, $32,130.13 for insurance premiums, $6,660 for towing charges, and $51,060 in refunds of security deposits, less a $6,042 reimbursement. It accepted documentary evidence supporting these payments. The court then considered whether Lim should have forfeited three security deposits totalling $45,500 and claimed additional lease payments upon early termination. Lim explained that the lessees were long-term repeat customers who had generated business and referrals for the Prime Cars Group, and that forfeiting deposits or pursuing further sums risked losing their future business. The court held that this explanation was commercially sensible and consistent with the principle that directors may take a broader view of the company's interests, including intangible benefits such as customer goodwill. It noted that Lim had forfeited a security deposit in another case where the lessee had repeatedly defaulted, demonstrating that she exercised judgment rather than following a blanket policy.

The court also addressed two transfers from Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd's DBS account to Lim's personal account ($5,516 and $4,000) that the plaintiff highlighted. It found that these transfers occurred in the context of Lim making substantial payments on behalf of the company and did not, in themselves, indicate dishonesty. Overall, the court concluded that Lim's decision to route the balance sale proceeds through her personal account was a response to the liquidity crisis created by the cessation of rental payments and the concealment of 10 cars, and that she used a significant portion of those funds to meet the company's obligations. The remaining portion was claimed as unpaid salary and transport allowance, which the court treated as a remuneration dispute rather than misappropriation.

The court was critical of the conduct of the Neo sisters and Heng Hong Hing, finding that they instructed Supreme Leasing & Limousine Pte Ltd to stop paying rentals to Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd and concealed 10 cars, fully aware that the company depended on those rentals to service its bank loans. It characterised their actions as the cause of the company's liquidity crisis and noted that they later used the resulting financial distress as the basis for claims against Lim. Nonetheless, the court confined its legal analysis to whether Lim's conduct met the statutory and fiduciary standards and held that she acted honestly and with reasonable diligence in managing the company's affairs during the crisis.

On the plaintiff's claims of dishonest assistance, knowing receipt, and conspiracy involving Lim and Zenith, the court found that the absence of any breach of duty by Lim meant that these derivative claims could not succeed. It further found no evidence of any agreement between Lim and Zenith's director to injure Prime Cars Leasing Pte Ltd, no unlawful act underpinning a conspiracy, and no suspicious circumstances that would have required Zenith to question Lim's authority. The court therefore dismissed all claims against Lim and proceeded to consider her counterclaim for reimbursement of expenses and remuneration, recognising that her documented expenditures on behalf of the company exceeded the net sale proceeds she had received.
